data.post_title: Ear Candles: What They Are, What They Claim, and What You Should Know
data.meta_title: Ear Candles: Are They Safe? | Raleigh Hearing & Tinnitus Center
data.meta_description: Are ear candles safe or effective? Learn the truth and safer alternatives. Schedule an ear evaluation with Raleigh Hearing & Tinnitus Center toda

featured_image.alt: Person lying on a treatment table while a practitioner performs ear candling

children.text: Ear candles are often marketed as a natural way to remove ear wax, but they are not supported by medical evidence.
Using ear candles can increase the risk of burns, blockage, and damage to the ear canal or eardrum.
Raleigh Hearing & Tinnitus Center offers professional ear cleaning and ear wax removal in Raleigh, NC.

Many people searching for ear care solutions come across ear candles online or through word of mouth. Ear candles are often described as a natural or gentle way to remove ear wax, improve hearing, or relieve pressure. For some, the idea feels comforting and simple. But hearing health is delicate, and not every popular remedy is safe or effective.

At Raleigh Hearing & Tinnitus Center, we believe people deserve clear, honest information about their ears. Understanding what ear candles are, how they work, and why professionals advise caution can help you make informed choices about your hearing and overall ear health.
What Are Ear Candles?
Ear candles, also called ear coning, are hollow tubes made from fabric soaked in wax or paraffin. One end is placed into the ear canal, and the other end is lit with a flame.

Supporters of ear candles claim that the heat creates suction, which pulls ear wax and debris out of the ear. Some also claim benefits such as improved hearing, relief from sinus pressure, or reduced ringing in the ears.
These claims sound appealing, especially to people dealing with discomfort or fullness in the ear. However, how something sounds and how it actually works are not always the same.
How Ear Candles Are Supposed to Work
The idea behind ear candles is that the flame creates a vacuum. This vacuum is said to draw wax and impurities out of the ear canal.
In reality, studies have shown that ear candles do not create enough suction to remove ear wax. Any residue seen after use usually comes from the candle itself, not from the ear.
The ear canal is a closed space. Heat and flame near the ear can create risk rather than benefit.
If you feel like you need to get your wax removed, see an audiologist for
professional ear wax removal
What Science Says About Ear Candles
Medical research does not support the use of ear candles for ear wax removal or hearing improvement.
Studies have found that:
No wax is removed from the ear canal
Candle residue can fall into the ear, causing blockage
Burns and injuries can occur
The eardrum can be damaged
Because of these risks, many medical organizations advise against using ear candles.
Risks of Using Ear Candles
While ear candles are often described as gentle, they can cause serious harm.
Possible risks include:
Burns to the ear, face, or scalp
Wax dripping into the ear canal
Increased blockage rather than removal
Punctured eardrum
Ear canal irritation or infection
These injuries can affect hearing and may require medical treatment to repair.
Ear Wax: What It Really Does
Ear wax is not dirt. It plays an important role in ear health.
Ear wax helps:
Trap dust and debris
Protect the ear canal from bacteria
Keep the skin in the ear canal from drying out
Most ears clean themselves naturally. Jaw movement from talking or chewing helps wax move outward over time.
Problems occur when wax builds up too much or becomes impacted.
Signs of Ear Wax Buildup
Excess ear wax can cause:
A feeling of fullness
Muffled hearing
Ringing in the ears
Discomfort or pressure
Trouble with hearing aids
When these symptoms appear, it is important to choose a safe solution.
Raleigh Hearing and Tinnitus Center connects you with
audiologists in Raleigh, NC
, who are trained to perform safe and gentle ear wax removal.
Why Ear Candles Do Not Remove Ear wax
The ear canal curves inward. Suction strong enough to pull wax out could damage the eardrum.
What people often see after ear candling is melted wax from the candle itself. This can be misleading and make it seem like wax was removed from the ear.
In reality, ear candles can push material deeper into the canal, making the blockage worse.
Ear Candles and Tinnitus Claims
Some people try ear candles to reduce ringing in the ears, also known as tinnitus.
Tinnitus usually comes from changes in the inner ear or auditory system, not from ear wax alone. Ear candles do not address the underlying causes of tinnitus and may increase irritation.
Professional evaluation is the safest way to understand tinnitus and explore treatment options.
Why Home Remedies Can Be Risky
Many home ear remedies focus on quick results rather than long-term health.
Risks of at-home treatments include:
Lack of proper diagnosis
Accidental injury
Delayed treatment for infections
Worsening symptoms
Ears are sensitive structures. Even small mistakes can lead to lasting problems.
Safe Alternatives to Ear Candles
If ear wax or ear discomfort is a concern, safer options exist.
These include:
Professional ear examinations
Clinically guided ear wax removal
Proper use of approved ear drops, when recommended
Education on ear care habits
Professional care focuses on safety, comfort, and hearing preservation.
How Audiologists Help with Ear Wax Concerns
Audiologists are trained to evaluate ear canal health and hearing.
At Raleigh Hearing & Tinnitus Center, care may include:
Visual inspection of the ear canal
Determining if wax is causing symptoms
Safe removal using approved tools
Guidance on prevention
This approach avoids the risks linked to ear candles.
Raleigh Hearing & Tinnitus Center offers professional ear cleaning and ear wax removal in Raleigh, NC.
Ear Candles and Children
Using ear candles on children is especially risky.
Children have:
Smaller ear canals
Thinner skin
Greater sensitivity to heat
Burns or blockage can happen quickly. Professional care is always recommended for children with ear concerns.
Ear Candles and Hearing Aids
For hearing aid users, ear candles pose an added risk.
Hearing aids already sit in the ear canal. Adding heat or wax can:
Damage hearing aid components
Increase moisture and blockage
Lead to discomfort or infection
Routine professional cleanings are safer and more effective.
Why Professional Guidance Matters
Ear symptoms often overlap. Fullness, hearing changes, ringing, or pain can come from many causes.
Only a proper evaluation can determine:
Whether ear wax is present
If infection or inflammation exists
Whether hearing loss is involved
What treatment is appropriate
Common Myths About Ear Candles

Myth: Ear candles are natural and safe
Fact: Natural does not always mean safe

Myth: Ear candles pull wax out
Fact: Studies show they do not

Myth: Ear candles help hearing
Fact: Hearing changes need a proper diagnosis
What to Do Instead of Using Ear Candles
If you are considering ear candles, pause and ask:
Do I know what is causing my symptoms?
Could this make things worse?
Is there a safer option?
Choosing professional care often saves time, discomfort, and stress.
When to Seek Professional Help
You should seek care if you notice:
Sudden hearing changes
Ongoing ear pain
Drainage from the ear
Ringing that does not go away
Trouble wearing hearing aids comfortably
These symptoms deserve attention, not guesswork.
Raleigh Hearing & Tinnitus Center’s Approach
Our goal is to help people hear clearly and feel confident about their care.
We focus on:
Patient education
Evidence-based treatment
Gentle, safe techniques
Long-term hearing health
We believe informed patients make better decisions.
Schedule A Professional Ear Wax Removal in Raleigh, NC Today
Ear candles may be easy to find, but they are not a safe or effective solution for ear wax, hearing concerns, or tinnitus. While they are often marketed as natural, the risks outweigh the benefits.
Before using ear candles or any home remedy, consider the value of professional guidance. Your ears play a vital role in how you connect with the world, and they deserve safe, thoughtful care.

At Raleigh Hearing & Tinnitus Center, we encourage people to choose care that protects their hearing and overall health. If you are experiencing ear fullness, blockage, or hearing changes, understanding the cause is the first step.
Schedule an appointment
today!

data.meta_title: Hearing Aids for Tinnitus | Raleigh Hearing & Tinnitus Center
data.meta_description: Hearing aids for tinnitus can reduce ringing and improve comfort through proper amplification and adjustment. Schedule a tinnitus evaluation today.

featured_image.alt: Person wearing glasses gently touching their ear, representing tinnitus symptoms and the use of hearing aids for relief

children.text: Hearing aids for tinnitus use gentle amplification and sound support to reduce awareness of ringing.
Tinnitus is affected by hearing loss, sound frequency, and how the brain processes volume and balance.
Raleigh Hearing & Tinnitus Center provides hearing aids for tinnitus in Raleigh, NC.

Many people searching for help with ringing in the ears ask about hearing aids for tinnitus. Tinnitus can sound like ringing, buzzing, humming, or clicking, and it can be frustrating, tiring, and distracting. For some people, tinnitus is mild. For others, it can affect sleep, focus, and daily life. While tinnitus does not have one cure, hearing aids for tinnitus can be a helpful device when used as part of professional care.

At Raleigh Hearing & Tinnitus Center, we help patients understand their tinnitus, explore treatment options, and find solutions that fit their lives. Hearing aids are often one part of a thoughtful, personalized plan.
What Is Tinnitus?

Tinnitus is the perception of sound when no outside sound is present. It is not a disease but a symptom. People experience tinnitus in different ways. Some hear ringing. Others hear buzzing, hissing, or pulsing sounds. Tinnitus can be constant or come and go.
Common causes of tinnitus include:
Hearing loss
Noise exposure
Changes in the inner ear and cochlea
Certain medications
Stress, fatigue, or changes in balance
In many cases, tinnitus is linked to hearing loss, even when hearing loss feels mild.
Why Hearing Loss and Tinnitus Are Often Connected

The brain expects sound input from the ears. Sound travels through the ear canal, into the middle ear, and then to the cochlea, where sound signals are sent to the brain. When hearing loss reduces that input, the brain may try to fill in the gaps.
This change in brain activity can affect:
Sound frequency processing
Perception of loudness or volume
Awareness of internal sounds
As a result, tinnitus may become more noticeable, especially in quiet settings.
How Hearing Aids Help with Tinnitus
Hearing aids do not remove tinnitus, but they can make it less noticeable and easier to manage. They help in several important ways.
1. Amplification of Everyday Sound
Hearing aids use gentle amplification to bring back soft sounds you may be missing, such as:
Speech
Environmental noise
Background sounds
When the brain receives more natural sound, tinnitus often fades into the background.
2. Reducing the Silence–Tinnitus Contrast
Tinnitus often feels louder in quiet places. By increasing sound input, hearing aids reduce the contrast between silence and tinnitus, which can lower awareness of the ringing.
3. Supporting Brain Balance
Improved sound input helps the brain stay focused on meaningful sound instead of internal noise. Over time, many people notice tinnitus feels less sharp or distracting.
Hearing Aids With Tinnitus Features
Many modern hearing aids include tinnitus support features. These features provide steady, low-level sound at specific frequencies to help calm the brain.
These features may include:
Soft white noise
Gentle tones
Nature sounds
The goal is not to cover tinnitus completely but to provide sound balance and relief. Proper adjustment of these features is key to comfort.
Are Hearing Aids for Tinnitus Right for Everyone?
Hearing aids for tinnitus are helpful for many people, but not for everyone.
They tend to help most when:
Tinnitus is linked to hearing loss
Quiet environments make tinnitus worse
Sound sensitivity or imbalance is present
A professional evaluation helps determine whether hearing aids are the right device for your tinnitus.
The Importance of a Tinnitus Evaluation
Tinnitus should never be treated with guesswork. A tinnitus evaluation helps answer important questions:
Is hearing loss present?
Are certain sound frequencies affected?
How does tinnitus impact focus, sleep, or balance?
Is medical follow-up needed?
At Raleigh Hearing & Tinnitus Center, we take time to explain results clearly and answer questions fully.
Hearing Aids Are One Part of Tinnitus Care
Hearing aids work best when paired with education and support. Tinnitus care often includes:
Hearing aids with tinnitus settings
Counseling and education
Sound therapy strategies
Aural rehabilitation
Sleep and stress guidance
This approach helps reduce frustration and improve daily comfort.
What to Expect When Using Hearing Aids for Tinnitus
Relief often happens gradually. As the brain adapts to new sound input, many people notice:
Less awareness of tinnitus
Improved focus in conversations
Better sound balance
Reduced stress around noise
Follow-up visits allow for ongoing adjustment to volume, frequency, and comfort.
Why Professional Fitting Matters
Hearing aids for tinnitus must be programmed carefully. Poor fitting can cause discomfort or feedback, which may worsen listening fatigue.
Professional care includes:
Matching amplification to hearing loss
Reducing feedback safely
Fine-tuning volume and frequency
Ongoing support as needs change
This level of care helps protect comfort and results. Contact us today to get expert care from an audiologist in Raleigh, NC.
Tinnitus and Sleep
Tinnitus often feels louder at night. While hearing aids help during the day, nighttime tools may include:
Sound machines
Tinnitus apps
Relaxation strategies
Your audiologist can help you choose safe options that support rest and balance.
Managing Expectations
Hearing aids for tinnitus do not cure tinnitus. They help reduce how much it affects your life.
Success often means:
Less distress
Better sleep
Improved concentration
Greater confidence
Relief looks different for each person.
Why Choose Raleigh Hearing & Tinnitus Center
Tinnitus can feel overwhelming, but you do not have to manage it alone.
We provide:
Comprehensive tinnitus evaluations
Advanced hearing aid devices
Personalized programming and adjustment
Ongoing support and education
Your care is guided by experience, clarity, and compassion.
Taking the First Step

If tinnitus is affecting your focus, sleep, or peace of mind, hearing aids for tinnitus may be part of the solution. The first step is understanding your hearing, your sound processing, and your options.

At Raleigh Hearing & Tinnitus Center, we help patients explore hearing aids for tinnitus with confidence and care. With the right device, proper amplification, and expert adjustment, tinnitus can become less overwhelming and easier to live with.
Contact us
today to schedule an appointment!

data.post_title: Lenire Side Effects, Insurance, and How It Compares With Hearing Aids
data.meta_title: Lenire: Side Effects, Insurance & Cost | Raleigh Hearing & Tinnitus Center
data.meta_description: Learn what to know about Lenire side effects, insurance questions, and how Lenire compares with hearing aids for tinnitus management.

featured_image.alt: Woman holding her ear in discomfort, representing ear pain or hearing concerns

children.text: Lenire is a prescription tinnitus treatment device that pairs sound through headphones with gentle tongue stimulation during each treatment session.
A full tinnitus assessment can identify possible causes, severity, and red flags, then guide the right plan for relief, which may include Lenire, hearing aids, sound therapy, or referral.
Raleigh Hearing & Tinnitus Center provides comprehensive tinnitus evaluations and customized Lenire care in Raleigh, NC.

If you are exploring tinnitus treatment options, you may have questions about Lenire, especially if you have already tried sound therapy, hearing aids, or general tinnitus management strategies. Lenire is a prescription tinnitus treatment device that combines sound stimulation through headphones with gentle tongue stimulation during each treatment session.

Because Lenire is different from a hearing aid, masking device, or app-based sound therapy, it is normal to wonder how it feels, whether side effects are possible, whether insurance may help, and how it fits into a broader tinnitus care plan.
Tinnitus can have more than one cause

Hearing loss is a common driver, but jaw tension, TMJ issues, stress, anxiety, certain medications, ear disease such as Meniere’s disease, and other causes can also play a role. That is why the right next step is a full tinnitus assessment before choosing a device or treatment approach.
This guide explains what to know before you ask whether Lenire is right for you.
What Is Lenire?

Lenire is a medical device designed to help temporarily relieve tinnitus symptoms in adults with at least moderate tinnitus. It is used at home after an evaluation, prescription, and fitting by a qualified provider experienced in tinnitus care.
The device has three main parts:
Headphones
The headphones deliver customized sound stimulation.
Tonguetip
The tonguetip rests gently on the tongue and provides mild electrical stimulation.
Controller
The controller coordinates the sound and tongue stimulation during treatment sessions.

The goal is not to cover up tinnitus in the moment like simple masking. Instead, Lenire is designed to support changes in how the brain responds to tinnitus over time. The long-term goal is tied to neuroplasticity, which is the brain’s ability to change its response patterns.
How Lenire Is Intended to Help Tinnitus

Tinnitus is an auditory signal with no outside sound source. For many people, the stress response and constant monitoring of the sound can increase distress. A structured program aims to reduce that distress over time.

One key idea in tinnitus care is habituation. Habituation occurs when your brain learns to treat the tinnitus signal as not urgent, so it fades into the background more often. Lenire is designed as a structured treatment approach to support that shift rather than simply masking the sound.

Outcomes vary, and that matters. Tinnitus can change with time of day, sleep quality, caffeine, noise exposure, and jaw tension. A strong plan tracks those variations and uses clear tools to measure change.
What Are Possible Lenire Side Effects?

Lenire is noninvasive, which means it does not involve surgery or medication. Clinical research submitted for FDA review reported no device-related serious adverse events, but side effects and day-to-day experiences can differ by individual.
Possible temporary effects may include:
Tongue sensation during treatment: You may notice tingling, pulsing, or mild stimulation during each treatment session. The provider sets the level and can adjust it based on comfort.

Temporary changes in tinnitus presence or awareness: Some patients report that tinnitus feels louder or more noticeable for a short period during the program. That does not always mean harm, but it should be shared with the provider so that settings and the session duration can be reviewed.
Listening fatigue: Daily sound input can feel tiring, especially early in the program.
Mouth or tongue discomfort: Any device that rests on the tongue can feel unusual at first.

A provider-led plan improves care because it includes follow-up adjustments. If the stimulation feels too strong, symptoms increase, or the schedule is hard to follow, the provider can update the program rather than asking you to push through discomfort.
What If I Take Medications for Nausea or Dizziness?
Some people have tinnitus with balance symptoms, nausea, or dizziness, including people with Meniere’s disease. Others have nausea from migraine conditions, illness, or medication effects.

If you take medications, especially for nausea or dizziness, follow your dosage instructions and precautions. Some medicines can cause side effects such as headache, drowsiness, upset stomach, rash, itching, swelling, or breathing problems. Medication interactions may also matter.
Do not change medications on your own. Consult your doctor about side effects, interactions, and whether any medication could be affecting the presence or severity of tinnitus.
Is Lenire Safe?

Lenire received FDA De Novo authorization as a prescription device for the temporary relief of tinnitus symptoms in adults with at least moderate tinnitus. It is intended to be used after prescription by a healthcare professional experienced in evaluating and managing tinnitus.
Safety also depends on patient selection. A tinnitus assessment helps determine whether Lenire is appropriate or whether another step should come first. Tinnitus can be connected to:
Hearing loss
Earwax blockage
Middle ear problems
Noise exposure
TMJ and jaw tension
Meniere’s disease and other inner ear disorders
Medication history
Stress, anxiety, and sleep disruption
Seek prompt medical guidance if tinnitus is sudden, one-sided, pulsatile, accompanied by sudden hearing loss, associated with dizziness, or linked with ear pain or drainage.
Is Lenire Covered by Insurance?

Insurance coverage for Lenire can vary. Some plans do not cover tinnitus devices the same way they cover diagnostic services or hearing-related benefits. Others may require documentation, a specific diagnosis form, prior authorization, or out-of-pocket payment.
Before starting, ask:
Is Lenire covered under my plan?
Do I need prior authorization?
Will the tinnitus assessment be billed separately from the device?
Can HSA or FSA funds be used?
What paperwork does the insurer need?
The best step is to contact the clinic’s insurance and billing team before you start.
Raleigh Hearing & Tinnitus Center provides comprehensive tinnitus evaluations and customized Lenire tinnitus care in Raleigh, NC.
Lenire vs. Hearing Aids for Tinnitus
Lenire and hearing aids can both be part of tinnitus care, but they address different needs.

Hearing aids can help when tinnitus is tied to hearing loss. By increasing access to everyday sounds, they can reduce the contrast between silence and tinnitus. Many modern hearing aids also include tinnitus sound support features.
Lenire is different. It is not worn all day for communication. It uses a structured program of paired sound and tongue stimulation.

For many people, hearing aids are the first step when hearing loss is present. For others, Lenire may be considered when tinnitus remains bothersome after hearing aids, sound therapy, or counseling-based strategies.
Lenire vs. Masking Devices
Masking devices and sound therapy tools use external sounds to reduce tinnitus awareness. They can provide short-term relief, especially for sleep or quiet rooms.
Lenire is designed for a different mechanism. It pairs sound with tongue stimulation during repeated sessions to support a longer-term shift in tinnitus response.
Some people use a mix of strategies, such as hearing aids for daily life, masking at night, and structured tinnitus therapy for long-term change.
Lenire vs. Traditional Tinnitus Treatments
Traditional tinnitus care may include:
Education and counseling
Hearing testing
Hearing aids
Sound therapy
Relaxation strategies to reduce anxiety
Sleep support
Treatment of related ear or hearing conditions
Lenire does not replace a full tinnitus evaluation. It belongs within a care plan that looks at causes, severity, and real-life impact.
Who May Be a Good Candidate for Lenire?

Lenire may be worth discussing if tinnitus is persistent, bothersome, and affecting quality of life. Candidate screening should confirm that there are no urgent red flags and that the plan fits your needs.
A strong candidate process includes:
A detailed tinnitus history
A tinnitus assessment and hearing evaluation
A review of medical red flags and related conditions, such as Meniere’s disease or TMJ issues
A discussion of goals and expected outcomes

Published Lenire clinical trial summaries report that many participants experienced benefit, and one commonly cited figure is that 83% of participants said they would recommend Lenire. However, results vary, and your provider should explain what the evidence does and does not mean for your specific case.
Questions to Ask Before Starting Lenire
Before beginning, ask your provider:
What did my tinnitus assessment show about cause and severity?
Do I also need hearing aids for hearing loss?
What side effects should I report right away?
How many times per day should I use Lenire, and how long should each session last?
What if I miss days, and could that affect outcomes?
How will we measure effectiveness, such as questionnaires, sleep, focus, or daily function?
What costs should I expect, and what does insurance require?
Learn More About Lenire in Raleigh, NC
Lenire is a prescription tinnitus treatment option that may help some adults with bothersome tinnitus. It is not the same as a hearing aid, masking device, or general sound therapy app.

If you are comparing Lenire with hearing aids, wondering about side effects, or trying to understand insurance questions, the best next step is a comprehensive tinnitus assessment with a provider trained in tinnitus care.

An evaluation can identify hearing loss, Meniere’s disease risk signs, TMJ factors, medication interactions, and other possible causes. It can also help you build a tinnitus management plan based on findings, not guesswork.
If you want clearer guidance and better long-term support, start with a tinnitus assessment.

children.text: Swimmer’s ear is a common outer ear infection often caused by water trapped in the ear canal after swimming.
Early symptoms like itchiness, pain, or blockage should not be ignored.
Raleigh Hearing & Tinnitus Center connects you to expert audiologists in Raleigh, NC, providing expert hearing care and guidance.

Many people first hear about swimmer’s ear after a long day at the pool, lake, or beach. While swimmers are at higher risk, swimmer’s ear can affect anyone. Warm weather, humidity, and trapped moisture in the ear canal can all play a role.
At Raleigh Hearing & Tinnitus Center, our
audiologists
help patients recognize swimmer’s ear early, understand why it happens, and know when professional care is needed to protect hearing and comfort.
What Is Swimmer’s Ear?
Swimmer’s ear is an infection of the outer ear canal, which is the passage that carries sound from the outside of the ear to the eardrum. The medical name for swimmer’s ear is
otitis externa
This infection often starts when water stays in the ear canal. The warm, dark space makes it easy for bacteria to grow, especially during hot weather or periods of high humidity.
Why Swimmer’s Ear Happens
The ear canal has natural defenses, including earwax, which helps keep moisture and bacteria out. When these defenses break down, infection can begin.
Common causes include:
Water trapped in the ear after swimming in a pool, lake, or ocean
Frequent exposure to humidity
Scratching the ear canal
Using cotton swabs or other objects in the ear
Wearing earbuds, hearing aids, or earplugs that trap moisture
Even a small scratch in the ear canal can allow bacteria to enter.
Who Is Most at Risk?
Anyone can get swimmer’s ear, but higher risk groups include:
Children and teens who swim often
Adults who spend a lot of time in water
Swimmers who do not dry their ears fully
People living in warm or humid climates
Individuals who clean their ears aggressively
You do not need to be a competitive swimmer to develop swimmer’s ear.
Early Symptoms of Swimmer’s Ear
Swimmer’s ear often begins with mild symptoms that can worsen quickly.
Early signs include:
Itchiness inside the ear
Mild ear pain
Redness of the ear canal
A feeling of fullness or blockage
At this stage, hearing may still seem normal, but discomfort is often present.
More Advanced Symptoms
If the infection progresses, symptoms may include:
Pain when touching or pulling the ear
Swelling that narrows the ear canal
Drainage from the ear
Muffled hearing caused by blockage
Pain when chewing or moving the jaw
These symptoms are a strong sign that it is time to see a doctor.
Can Swimmer’s Ear Affect Hearing?
Yes. Swelling and fluid in the ear canal can block sound from reaching the eardrum. This can cause:
Temporary hearing loss
A plugged or full sensation
Distorted or muffled sound
Once the infection clears, hearing usually returns to normal.
Swimmer’s Ear vs. Other Ear Infections
Swimmer’s ear affects the outer ear canal, not the middle ear.
Key differences:
Swimmer’s ear pain gets worse when the ear is touched
Middle ear infections often cause pressure or fever
Swimmer’s ear is more common after water exposure
A proper exam helps identify the correct condition.
Raleigh Hearing and Tinnitus Center connects you to
audiologists in Raleigh, NC
providing ear check-ups and comprehensive hearing tests.
What Not to Do
Some home remedies can make swimmer’s ear worse.
Avoid:
Using cotton swabs
Putting drops in the ear without guidance
Using alcohol or peroxide in a painful ear
Ignoring pain, swelling, or drainage
These actions can increase irritation and delay healing.
How Swimmer’s Ear Is Diagnosed
A doctor diagnoses swimmer’s ear by examining the ear canal. They look for:
Redness and swelling
Drainage
Tenderness
In some cases, hearing may be checked to rule out other issues.
Treatment for Swimmer’s Ear
Treatment depends on how severe the infection is.
Common treatments include:
Prescription ear drops
Keeping the ear dry
Avoiding swimming during healing
Pain relief as recommended
In more serious cases, antibiotics may be prescribed to clear the infection.
Why Seeing a Doctor Matters
Seeing a doctor early can:
Reduce pain faster
Prevent the infection from spreading
Shorten recovery time
Protect hearing health
People with diabetes or weakened immune systems should seek care right away.
Preventing Swimmer’s Ear
Prevention focuses on keeping the ear canal dry and protected.
Helpful tips include:
Dry ears gently after swimming
Tilt your head to drain water
Avoid putting objects in the ear
Remove wet earbuds or hearing aids
Use swim earplugs if recommended
Custom earplugs can help frequent swimmers reduce water exposure safely.
Swimmer’s Ear and Hearing Aids
Hearing aid users should be careful with moisture. Trapped moisture can:
Irritate the ear canal
Increase bacteria growth
Lead to discomfort or infection
Proper cleaning and drying routines are important.
Children and Swimmer’s Ear
Children are more likely to get swimmer’s ear due to:
Smaller ear canals
Frequent swimming
Difficulty describing symptoms
Watch for ear pulling, crying, or trouble sleeping after swimming.
Frequently Asked Questions
Are home remedies effective for swimmer’s ear?

Home remedies may help prevent swimmer’s ear in some cases, but they are not a reliable treatment once an infection has started. Using household items in the ear can sometimes make symptoms worse or delay proper care. If you have pain, drainage, or ongoing itchiness, it’s best to see a doctor for safe and effective treatment.
Is swimmer’s ear contagious?

No, swimmer’s ear is not contagious. It cannot be spread from person to person, even with skin contact. The infection develops when moisture, bacteria, or irritation affect the ear canal, often after swimming or exposure to humidity.
How serious is swimmer’s ear?

Swimmer’s ear is usually mild when treated early, but it can become more serious if ignored. Without proper care, swelling and blockage can increase pain and affect hearing. In rare cases, the infection can spread to nearby tissue. Seeing a doctor early helps prevent complications and speeds healing.
How to relieve swimmer’s ear pain?

Pain relief starts with keeping the ear dry and avoiding pressure or touching the ear. Over-the-counter pain relievers may help, but the most effective relief often comes from prescription ear drops that reduce infection and inflammation. If pain worsens or does not improve within a day or two, a doctor should evaluate the ear.
How Raleigh Hearing & Tinnitus Center Can Help
While swimmer’s ear often requires medical treatment, audiology care supports overall ear health.
We help by:
Evaluating ear canal health
Identifying moisture or wax blockage
Supporting hearing aid users during healing
Guiding patients on when to see a doctor
Our focus is comfort, safety, and long-term hearing wellness.
